coredata.dc:title: Self-polymerized polydopamine-imprinted layer-coated carbon dots as a fluorescent sensor for selective and sensitive detection of 17β-oestradiol
coredata.dc:creator: Bhogal, Shikha
Mohiuddin, Irshad
Kumar, Sandeep
Malik, Ashok Kumar
Kim, Ki-Hyun
Kaur, Kuldeep
coredata.dc:description: The compound 17β-oestradiol (E2) is a steroidal oestrogen used extensively in food processing and animal husbandry. As E2 is well-known as a typical endocrine disrupting chemical, its release, penetration, and exposure create serious environmental concerns. Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted great attention due to their excellent fluorescent and non-toxic properties. To help improve the selectivity of CDs, they can be combined with molecularly-imprinted polymers (MIPs). In light of the limitations involved in the fabrication of MIP layer on CDs (e.g., time consumption and low controllability of imprinted layer), the mussel inspired dopamine self-polymerization can be considered as an alternative option. As functional monomer in molecular imprinted technology, dopamine can be used efficiently to polymerize in weak alkaline condition (e.g., formation of polydopamine). In this research, a new method was developed for selective and sensitive fluorescent detection of E2 based on self-polymerization of dopamine (functional monomer) on fluorescent carbon dots (CDs@MI-PDA). The developed sensor selectively binds with E2 to quench the fluorescence intensity of CDs by photo-induced electron transfer. The sensor showcases a detection limit of E2 as 0.34 ng/mL with a linearity over 1–50 ng/mL. Furthermore, the probe was successfully applied to water (tap and river water) and milk samples with recoveries of 96.4–102.2 %. This study is expected to open a new path for the development of a simple and convenient detection approach for E2 present in complex matrices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Polydopamine
Oestradiol
Fluorescence detection
Imprinting method

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of polydopamine-coated molecularly-imprinted carbon dots (CDs@MI-PDA)
Synthesis and possible detection mechanism of the CDs@MI-PDA sensor
Optimized conditions for the CDs@MI-PDA sensor
Recognition performance and sensitivity of the CDs@MI-PDA sensor
Practical application of the sensor and performance comparison

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning
Department of Chemistry, University of York

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors (SB, IM, SK, and AKM) acknowledge the UGC-SAP and Chemistry Department, Punjabi University, Patiala, for providing lab and instrument facilities. AKM thanks the UGC, New Delhi for a Mid-career award grant. KK thanks Mata Gujri College, Fatehgarh Sahib, Punjab for providing lab facilities. KHK acknowledges support made by a grant from the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science and ITC (MSIT) of Korean government (Grant No: 2021R1A3B1068304).

The authors (SB, IM, SK, and AKM) acknowledge the UGC-SAP and Chemistry Department, Punjabi University, Patiala, for providing lab and instrument facilities. AKM thanks the UGC, New Delhi for a Mid-career award grant. KK thanks Mata Gujri College, Fatehgarh Sahib, Punjab for providing lab facilities. KHK acknowledges support made by a grant from the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science and ITC (MSIT) of Korean government (Grant No: 2021R1A3B1068304).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the synthesis procedures for polydopamine-imprinted coatings over CDs (CDs@MI-PDA) for selective fluorescence detection of 17β-oestradiol.
FTIR spectra of materials prepared for the sensing of E2: (a) CDs and (b) CDs@MI-PDA.

Morphology, size, and nature of synthesized materials evaluated by high-resolution electron microscopy (HRTEM). I) HRTEM images (e.g., morphology and size) of (a and b) CDs with a spherical morphology and (c and d) CDs@MI-PDA with a spherical morphology and uniform coating of the PDA layer on CDs. II) The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns of (e) CDs showing diffused rings to comply with the amorphous nature and (f) CDs@MI-PDA with sharp diffraction indicates crystalline nature.

Fluorescence emission spectra of synthesized fluorescent materials: CDs@MI-PDA with E2, CDs@MI-PDA without E2, and CDs@NI-PDA. Excitation wavelength: 420 nm; emission wavelength: 555 nm; both excitation and emission slit widths: 10 nm.

Fluorescence emission spectra of synthesized materials by adding different concentrations of E2 in the range of 0–50 ng/mL: (a) CDs@MI-PDA and (b) CDs@NI-PDA. Excitation wavelength: 420 nm; emission wavelength: 555 nm; both excitation and emission slit widths: 10 nm.

Stern-Volmer plot of materials synthesized by adding different concentration levels of E2 in the range of 0–50 ng/mL: (a) CDs@MI-PDA and (b) CDs@NI-PDA. Excitation wavelength: 420 nm; emission wavelength: 555 nm; and both excitation and emission slit widths: 10 nm. The error bars indicate the standard deviation of three replicate measurements.
Determination of E2 in different samples (tap water, river water, milk, and chicken samples) by the proposed CDs@MI-PDA sensor.
Performance comparison of the developed sensor (CDs@MI-PDA) with other reported methods (electrochemical, HPLC, and fluorescence).

row.entry: River water and raw milk samples

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: MWCNT/AuNP/GCE - gold nanoparticle multi-walled carbon nanotube-modified glass carbon electrode; MCPE-MMIP - magneto carbon paste electrode based on magnetic molecularly-imprinted polymer; Pt/Pol/HRP - conducting polymer and horseradish peroxidase-modified platinum electrode; SM-MIMs - zipper-like on/off-switchable and magnetic molecularly-imprinted microspheres; MYS-MMIPs - mesoporous yolk shell structure magnetic molecularly-imprinted polymers; and RAM-MIP - restricted access media molecularly-imprinted polymers.

head.ce:title: Self-polymerized polydopamine-imprinted layer-coated carbon dots as a fluorescent sensor for selective and sensitive detection of 17β-oestradiol

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Punjabi University, Patiala 147002, Punjab, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Punjabi University, Patiala 147002, Punjab, India

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, Hanyang University, Seoul 04763, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Mata Gujri College, Fatehgarh Sahib 140407, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Mata Gujri College, Fatehgarh Sahib 140407, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The compound 17β-oestradiol (E2) is a steroidal oestrogen used extensively in food processing and animal husbandry. As E2 is well-known as a typical endocrine disrupting chemical, its release, penetration, and exposure create serious environmental concerns. Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted great attention due to their excellent fluorescent and non-toxic properties. To help improve the selectivity of CDs, they can be combined with molecularly-imprinted polymers (MIPs). In light of the limitations involved in the fabrication of MIP layer on CDs (e.g., time consumption and low controllability of imprinted layer), the mussel inspired dopamine self-polymerization can be considered as an alternative option. As functional monomer in molecular imprinted technology, dopamine can be used efficiently to polymerize in weak alkaline condition (e.g., formation of polydopamine). In this research, a new method was developed for selective and sensitive fluorescent detection of E2 based on self-polymerization of dopamine (functional monomer) on fluorescent carbon dots (CDs@MI-PDA). The developed sensor selectively binds with E2 to quench the fluorescence intensity of CDs by photo-induced electron transfer. The sensor showcases a detection limit of E2 as 0.34 ng/mL with a linearity over 1–50 ng/mL. Furthermore, the probe was successfully applied to water (tap and river water) and milk samples with recoveries of 96.4–102.2 %. This study is expected to open a new path for the development of a simple and convenient detection approach for E2 present in complex matrices.

ce:section.ce:para: In a wide variety of assays and/or sensor systems, many forms of bio-receptors (e.g., enzymes and antibodies) have been used as the recognition element (Yemiş et al., 2013). The utility of such receptors is nonetheless limited by several demerits in many occasions (e.g., relatively poor stability, short shelf life, high cost, and weak immobility) (Yemiş et al., 2013). In this respect, molecularly-imprinted polymers (MIPs) can be recommended as a highly useful option for the build up of artificial recognition materials as they can be employed to favorably simulate the function of biological receptors. The superiority of MIPs over natural receptors can be explained in terms of their merits (e.g., chemical stability, durability, high reusability, and low production costs) (BelBruno, 2018). Further, because of high practicability, MIPs have been rapidly applied in many fields such as separation science, drug delivery, bio/chemosensing, catalysis, artificial antibodies, and degradation (Chen et al., 2016; Speltini et al., 2017; Bhogal et al., 2020). The surface imprinting strategy is often recognized as a useful option to overcome the limitations of traditional MIPs (e.g., incomplete template removal, slow mass transfer, and low rebinding capacity) (Ensafi et al., 2017). In surface imprinting, imprinted layers are fabricated on support materials (Fe3O4, silica, quantum dots, and TiO2) to facilitate the removal and adsorption of diverse target molecules (e.g., template-like bisphenol A, tetracycline, and ketoprofen) (Niu et al., 2016; Bhogal et al., 2019).

The formation of MIP-based fluorescent sensors is a desirable option to offer high selectivity and sensitivity with the aid of fluorescence detection. Among the fluorescent probes, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted great popularity because of their unique optical properties. CDs can showcase high performance as fluorescent probes with many meritful properties (e.g., high photo-stability, excellent biocompatibility, and chemical inertness) compared to conventional organic dyes and/or semiconductor quantum dots (Xia et al., 2019). The most common method used for fabrication of an MIP layer on CDs involves free radical or sol gel polymerization (Feng et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2018). However, as such methods are based on complicated synthesis procedures (e.g., time consuming and low controllability), it is desirable to develop rapid, simple, and controllable methods for the synthesis of CD-based MIP sensors.

Dopamine (DA) is a natural biomolecule present in mussel adhesive proteins and can be used for polymerization (e.g., formation of poly-dopamine (PDA) in alkaline solution at room temperature) (Liu et al., 2020). Because of the self-polymerization ability of DA, PDA has great potential for application in molecular imprinting technology (Hu et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2020). The mechanical strength of PDA is also excellent due to the presence of a benzene ring structure (Li et al., 2017), while PDA coatings can accommodate diverse template molecules with the aid of multiple types of functional groups (such as amine, indole, catechol, and quinine) (Wang et al., 2018). As such, dopamine is regarded as an ideal functional monomer for surface imprinting.

To demonstrate the utility of CDs and PDA, a fluorescent sensor was developed by imprinting a PDA film on the surface of CDs as a fluorescent probe (CDs@MI-PDA) and used for selective sensing of 17β-oestradiol (E2). As a steroidal oestrogen, E2 has been used extensively in food processing and animal husbandry (owing to its enhanced potential in promoting the growth of female reproductive organs and in reducing the production cost of meat and dairy products). As a typical endocrine-disrupting chemical (EDC), E2 has been detected in surface waters in the range of ng L−1 to mg L−1 (Zhang et al., 2018). As E2 can enter the human body or other organisms, it can also interfere with normal endocrine functions and cause detrimental effects such as blood brain barrier damage, male infertility, increased incidence of obesity, and breast and testis cancers (Tian et al., 2020). Therefore, it is of great concern to properly monitor the presence of E2 in environmental and/or food samples for its efficient control or mitigation.

In this study, a core shell nano-composite consisting of a PDA imprinting layer and CDs as the core was prepared and applied for specific recognition and quantitation of E2 as the main target. To begin with, the synthesis of CDs was carried out using dextrose and self-polymerization of dopamine around CDs in the presence of E2 in a weak alkaline solution. This preparation is a simple, green, and economical route for their synthesis. Moreover, the obtained nano-composite could have a thin imprinting shell with specific recognition sites for E2 with fast binding kinetics. To the best of our knowledge, this study can be regarded as the first attempt for the construction of a CD-embedded PDA imprinting layer for the application toward the specific recognition of E2.

The compounds 17β-oestradiol (E2), diethylstilbestrol (DES), 17α-ethynylestradiol (E3), bisphenol A (BPA), diethyl phthalate (DEP), and levofloxacin (LEV) were procured from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Dextrose was obtained from Merck (Mumbai, India). Phosphoric acid was obtained from Loba Chemie (Mumbai, India). Dopamine was supplied from HiMedia (Mumbai, India). A 1 mg/mL standard stock solution of E2 was prepared in methanol and stored at 4 °C. The working standard solutions of E2 were prepared daily in the range of 1–50 ng/mL by further dilution of the standard stock solution with triply distilled water (TDW: SG Works, India), followed by sonication for degassing prior to use.

The size, elemental composition, and nature of the synthesized material phases (CDs vs CDs@MI-PDA) were analysed by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) imaging equipped with energy dispersive x-ray (EDX) on a Model JEM 2100 plus (JEOL, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) analysis was carried out using RXIFT-IR (Perkin Elmer, Japan). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out using a Hitachi STA7300 thermogravimetric analyser to acquire thermal degradation curves of the synthesized materials (CDs and CDs@MI-PDA). The weight loss data for those samples were recorded in the range from 35 to 550 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C/min with a continuous supply of nitrogen. The fluorescence spectra were obtained by a Shimadzu RF-5301 PC spectrofluorophotometer equipped with a quartz cell. The slit widths of both excitation and emission monochromators were 10 nm with an excitation wavelength of 420 nm. UV–Vis absorption spectra were obtained by a UV–Vis 1600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The pH of the solutions was measured using a Delux pH meter 101 (Electronics India). An REMI RM-12C centrifuge (REMI Laboratory Instruments, India) was used to centrifuge the samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of polydopamine-coated molecularly-imprinted carbon dots (CDs@MI-PDA)

ce:section.ce:para: The CDs were synthesized by a microwave assisted method. For this, 1 g of dextrose was dissolved in 20 mL of water in a beaker. Further, 4 mL of concentrated phosphoric acid (as dehydrating agent) was added in the above solution. This mixture was then stirred for 1 min and put in the centre of the rotation plate of a microwave oven for about 2 min. The change of the solution colour from transparent to brown indicates carbonization of the reactant (dextrose). After cooling to room temperature, the obtained brown solution was centrifuged for 10 min (10,000 rpm). The brown supernatant containing CDs was collected and filtered through a PTFE syringe filter. The purified brown coloured CDs solution was stored at 4 °C until further use.

The CDs@MI-PDA was synthesized through self-polymerization of dopamine on the surface of CDs. First, 5 mL of the as-prepared CDs (Section 2.3.1) was mixed with 10 mL of tris buffer (10 mM, pH = 8) and 20 mL of ethanol, followed by sonication for 10 min with the aid of ultrasonic bath (Sarthak Services, Ambala, India). Afterwards, 20 mg of E2 and 80 mg of dopamine were added to the above solution. Then, the whole reaction mixture was stirred continuously at room temperature for 12 h. The resultant imprinted particles were collected by centrifugation for 10 min (10,000 rpm). The obtained product was washed with TDW and then with MeOH:acetic acid (95:5;v/v) to extract the template (E2). Similarly, non-imprinted particles (CDs@NI-PDA) were synthesized by the same procedure but without the template (as a control). Finally, the obtained products were dried in an oven (60 °C for 6 h) and then further used for characterization and fluorescence studies.

In a volumetric flask, 150 μL of the CDs@MI-PDA solution (1 mg in 10 mL water), 500 μL of phosphate buffer solution (pH 7), and 1 mL of E2 solution of different concentrations (1–50 ng/mL) were combined. This mixture was diluted to 5 mL with TDW. After incubation for 10 min, the fluorescent emission intensity of the solution excited by 420 nm light was measured at 555 nm and marked as F. Similarly, the fluorescence emission intensity of the blank sample (without addition of E2) was measured and marked as F0. The change of the fluorescent intensity was defined as ΔF (ΔF = F0 − F).

The water samples include tap water from the laboratory and river water collected from the Tawi River (Jammu, J & K, India) during the months of November and December 2020. All of the water samples were collected in glass bottles and stored at 4 °C until analysis. Samples of three milk brands and chicken were purchased from a local market (Patiala) and stored at 4 °C before analysis. The 5 mL milk and 5 g chicken (homogenized by using laboratory blender) samples were added separately to a 20 mL ACN solution for extraction of target analyte (E2) in a closed tube. Further, they were subjected to ultra-sonication for 15 min, and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min. The extracted supernatants of the milk and chicken samples were then diluted with TDW. Before the analysis, all of the samples were filtered through Whatman grade 1 filter paper to remove any suspended particles, followed by purification with a syringe filter (0.22 μm). All samples were tested by the CDs@MI-PDA sensor before adding the E2 solution. Then, the milk and water samples were spiked with an E2 stock solution with various concentrations of 1, 5, and 10 ng/mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and possible detection mechanism of the CDs@MI-PDA sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis process of the CDs@MI-PDA sensor involves the fabrication of CDs with dopamine (as a functional monomer) in the presence of E2 to lead the formation of CDs@MI-PDA core-shell nano-composites. It is believed that adhesion of PDA to the substrate can occur either through covalent oxidative polymerization or through non-covalent self-assembly (Batul et al., 2017). In the synthesized CDs@MI-PDA, dopamine appears to bind to the surface of CDs with catechols by forming hydrogen bonding and/or π-π stacking (Jalili and Amjadi, 2018). Afterwards, it becomes spontaneously self-polymerized under alkaline conditions to form a polydopamine layer around the CDs. During self-polymerization, E2 molecules are embedded in the cross-linked polymeric network through non-covalent interactions of the amine and hydroxyl groups on the PDA chain. It is supposed that the dopamine with amino, hydroxyl, and phenyl groups can interact with E2 by forming multi‑hydrogen bonds, π-π interactions, and through hydrophobic interactions. After removal of E2, imprinted cavities are obtained in the CDs@MI-PDA (Fig. 1 ). This type of preparation process can help avoid the tedious procedures conventionally required for preparation of CD-based MIPs. Moreover, this green synthesis process proceeds quite efficiently and conveniently when DA is applied as both the monomer and cross-linker during the imprinting procedure.

Fluorescence quenching of the sensor occurs with formation of hydrogen bonds between CDs@MI-PDA and E2. The photo-induced electron transfer from excited state CDs to E2 might be responsible for the fluorescence quenching. The fluorescence in the CDs is expected to arise from the radiative recombination of electron-hole pairs. In the absence of E2, a fluorescence emission is generated at 555 nm. After addition of E2, the amine and hydroxyl groups in the binding sites of MI-PDA can interact with the analyte through H-bonding and van der Waals forces. Note that the UV absorption band of E2 is close to the band gap of CDs (Fig. S1a). The fluorescence quenching of CDs occurs as the electrons in the conduction band of excited CDs are transferred to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals of E2. Moreover, because there is no spectral overlap between CDs@MI-PDA and E2, the fluorescence quenching of CDs should be ascribed to electron transfer between CDs and E2 (Fig. S1b).

The functional groups on the surface of CDs and CDs@MI-PDA were characterized by FTIR. With CDs, the main absorption peaks at 3452, 2924, 1586, and 1078 cm−1 can be assigned to OH, CH, CC, and COC stretching vibrations, respectively (Fig. 2a). On the other hand, the FT-IR spectrum of CDs@MI-PDA shows characteristic bands of catechol and amine groups, confirming fabrication of a PDA layer on the CDs (Fig. 2b). The bands at 3432, 3312, 1609, and 1497 cm−1 can be assigned to the OH stretch, NH stretch, superposition of NH bending and phenylic CC stretch, and aromatic CH bending vibration, respectively, to confirm the existence of the PDA layer on the CDs.

HR-TEM analysis was carried out to investigate the morphology and size of the as-synthesized CDs and CDs@MI-PDA. As shown in Fig. 3a and b, CDs are uniform with a spherical shape (average diameter of 5–8 nm). The CDs@MI-PDA had a similar morphology (spherical) to a PDA layer uniformly coated on CDs (Fig. 3c and d). The MI-PDA layer could be estimated to be 10–15 nm thick. This thickness of the imprinting layer would be effective for mass transport processes between the solution and CDs@MI-PDA surface.

The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) was carried out to learn more about the nature of the phase of the synthesized materials. The SAED patterns of CDs with diffuse rings suggest an amorphous nature of carbon dots (Fig. 3e). On the other hand, sharp diffraction spots in the SAED pattern of CDs@MI-PDA indicate the crystalline nature of CDs@MI-PDA (Fig. 3f). EDX analysis was also conducted to characterize the elemental composition of CDs and CDs@MI-PDA. Accordingly, the synthesized materials were found to contain C, O, and N elements. In the spectra of CDs, only C and O are present (Fig. S2a). However, after self-polymerization of DA on the CDs, the N peak appeared to reflect the N element of the amino group in DA (Fig. S2b). These results demonstrate successful formation of a PDA layer on the surface of CDs.

TGA was performed to assess the thermal stability of the materials in the range of 35-550 °C (Fig. S3). For CDs, the first weight loss (1.2 %) was observed below 170 °C due to evaporation of physically absorbed water molecules. The second weight loss of 11.4 % was observed between 285 and 320 °C, which might reflect the loss of groups attached through weak interactions in the CDs. The final loss of 7.3 % between 428 and 500 °C might be attributed to decomposition of organic functional groups. On the other hand, CDs@MI-PDA showed a first weight loss of 5.3 % below 160 °C possibly due to the loss of water molecules. The second weight loss (32.2 %) at 200–350 °C might be ascribable to thermal decomposition of the PDA layer and molecular chain. These results confirmed that the PDA layer was successfully grafted on the surface of CDs.

The fluorescence spectrum of the CDs@MI-PDA was evaluated under excitation wavelengths from 320 to 500 nm. As shown in Fig. S4, the CDs@MI-PDA exhibits an excitation-independent fluorescence centred at 555 nm. As the strongest emission peak was observed at an excitation wavelength of 420 nm, it was selected for further studies. CDs@NI-PDA shows strong fluorescence emission, while CDs@MI-PDA with E2 shows a weak fluorescent signal. However, after removal of E2, the fluorescence intensity of CDs@MI-PDA was restored and was comparable to that of the pristine form (Fig. 4 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimized conditions for the CDs@MI-PDA sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve highly sensitive detection of E2, it is necessary to optimize the key parameters that influence the interaction of E2 with the CDs@MI-PDA sensor (e.g., response time, pH, amount, and fluorescence stability).

To investigate the kinetics of the sensor, the fluorescence intensity was measured at different times after adding E2 (15 ng/mL) to a 5 mL solution containing 150 μL of CDs@MI-PDA. As shown in Fig. S5a, upon the addition of E2, the fluorescence intensity difference (F0 − F) changed rapidly for up to 10 min and remained constant afterwards. The fast kinetics can be attributed to the presence of imprinted cavities on the sensor surface as E2 can readily access these sites without any resistance in mass transfer. Hence, 10 min was selected as the incubation time.

The effect of the amount of CDs@MI-PDA on the fluorescence quenching efficiency caused by E2 was investigated by changing the volume of CDs@MI-PDA from 10 to 150 μL. The highest quenching effect was observed at 150 μL (Fig. S5b). The fluorescence intensity for larger amounts (>150 μL) of CDs@MI-PDA is greater than the spectrophotometer is capable of recording. Therefore, 150 μL was chosen as the optimum amount of CDs@MI-PDA for further experiments.

The pH of the solution plays an important role in the sensing as it not only influences the surface charge of the sensor, but also affects the ionization of analytes in the solution. To fully understand the effect of pH on the fluorescence quenching efficiency of the CDs@MI-PDA sensor, the pH was controlled in the range of 3–11. Next, 5 mL solutions containing CDs@MI-PDA (150 μL) and E2 (15 ng/mL) were prepared at different pH values using 0.1 M solutions of HCl or NaOH. After 10 min, the fluorescence was measured at room temperature. The maximum fluorescence quenching was observed in the pH range of 6–7 (Fig. S5c). This can be explained by considering the pKa value of E2 and the pHpzc value of the PDA layer. At pH values <4, the PDA layer is positively charged (pHpzc of PDA is 4) to support moderate permeability and interactions with anionic species (Socas-Rodríguez et al., 2015). The pKa values of oestrogens are around 7–11 in ionized forms due to protonation of hydroxyl groups of E2 at pH <4 (Socas-Rodríguez et al., 2015). Consequently, the H-bonding between the CDs@MI-PDA and E2 weakened to reduce quenching. On the other hand, at high pH (<7), PDA is negatively charged to result in a net negative charge of E2 (Socas-Rodríguez et al., 2015). As a result, the repulsion between the PDA layer and E2 can prevent penetration of analyte to binding sites that would otherwise suppress the quenching. Therefore, the solution pH was maintained at 7 to obtain the maximum fluorescence quenching.

The fluorescence stability of the CDs@MI-PDA sensor was examined by repeated detection of the fluorescence emission intensity every 5 min. The results shown in Fig. S5d reveal a stable emission of CDs@MI-PDA within 60 min. This reveals the good physical stability and uniform dispersion of CDs@MI-PDA in the solution. Moreover, repeated detection of the fluorescence intensity was also recorded after one month. As the CDs@MI-PDA sensor retained 95.6 % of its original fluorescence emission intensity, the storage stability of the fluorescent sensor should be satisfactory.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recognition performance and sensitivity of the CDs@MI-PDA sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The ability of the synthesized CDs@MI-PDA fluorescent probe to quantitatively measure E2 was evaluated. Under the optimized conditions (pH = 7; incubation time = 10 min; amount of CDs@MI-PDA = 150 μL), the recognition performance of the proposed sensor was assessed with addition of different concentrations (1–50 ng/mL) of E2. The fluorescence intensity decreased with increasing concentration of E2 (Fig. 5a). Good linearity was observed in the range from 1 to 50 ng/mL with a correlation coefficient of 0.986 and an RSD below 3 % (N = 3). In contrast, a decrease of the fluorescence intensity was not apparent for the corresponding CDs@NI-PDA at the same concentration of E2 (Fig. 5b). It is clearly seen from the comparison of Fig. 5a and b that the decrease of the fluorescence intensity of CDs@MI-PDA is much higher than that of CDs@NI-PDA. This indicates that CDs@MI-PDA could greatly enhance the quenching efficiency to enhance the spectral sensitivity to E2 with the formation of specific recognition sites for E2.

The phenomenon of fluorescence quenching is expressed by the Stern-Volmer equation:
F 0
F
= 1 +
K SV
C

Here, F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of the template (E2), respectively; [C] is the concentration of E2; and KSV is the quenching constant for E2. As illustrated in Fig. 6a and b, CDs@MI-PDA and CDs@NI-PDA exhibited different Stern-Volmer relationships in the linear range from 1 to 50 ng/mL. The decrease of fluorescence intensity of CDs@MI-PDA was much larger than that of CDs@NI-PDA at the same E2 concentration. To demonstrate the imprinting effect, an imprinting factor (IF) defined as the ratio of KSV,MIP to KSV,NIP was calculated. The high IF value of 8.5 confirmed the higher selectivity of E2 towards CDs@MI-PDA than CDs@NI-PDA.

The limit of detection (LOD) of 0.34 ng/mL meets the requirement for trace analysis. The LOD was calculated based on the ratio of
3 δ
b
. Here, δ is the standard deviation of the blank signal, while b is the slope of the calibration curve.

The selectivities of the CDs@MI-PDA and CDs@NI-PDA sensors were evaluated by a binding test using DES, E3, BPA, DEP, and LEVO. The quenching efficiency of CDs@MI-PDA was higher for E2 than for the other molecules (Fig. S6). However, the structure of E3, which is quite similar to that of E2, can cause a stronger quenching effect than the other competitive molecules although it is still lower than that of E2. On the other hand, CDs@NI-PDA exhibited similar selectivities between E2 and other molecules. This phenomenon can be explained by formation of specific recognition sites complementary with the template molecule in terms of the shape, size, and functionality generated on CDs@MI-PDA. Hence, the E2 template could be strongly rebound to CDs@MI-PDA to produce significant fluorescence quenching. However, the other molecules with different shapes and molecular weights than E2 were not complementary to the recognition sites. As such, they were unable to quench the CDs fluorescence, although they were able to interact with PDA by H-bonding.

In addition, the effects of possible interferences were studied by considering the list of cations (e.g., Na+, K+, Ca2+, Al3+, Mn2+, Mg2+ Zn2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Cr3+), anions (e.g., Cl‐, SO4 2‐, NO3 ‐, CO3 2‐, CH3COO‐), and some organic compounds (e.g., glucose, urea, sucrose, uric acid, and ascorbic acid) that can be present in real samples along with the target, i.e., E2. Hence, fluorescence analysis was performed after adding the interfering species (at varying concentration levels) to an E2 solution as described in Section 2.4. The tolerance limit was taken as the concentration of added interfering species that can cause the variation of ±5 % to the fluorescence signal. As summarized in Table S1, most species including cations (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Al3+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, and Fe2+) and anions (Cl‐, SO4 2‐, NO3 ‐, and CO3 2‐) did not interfere in the analysis of E2 even when they are present at 1000 times greater than E2. For other species, interfering effects were not evident up to 500 (Zn2+, Cr3+, and CH3COO‐), 250 (glucose, sucrose, and urea), and 100 times (ascorbic acid and uric acid). As such, interferences are unlikely to exert much effect on the sensing of E2 by CDs@MI-PDA.

The ability to reproduce the CDs@MI-PDA sensor is important for upscaling of this technique. Reproducibility is also an important factor in determining the synthetic viability of the method. To assess the reproducibility (e.g., in terms of synthesis and related variables), the CDs@MI-PDA sensor was investigated over one week by preparing it in seven batches under identical conditions as described in Section 2.3. The as-prepared CDs@MI-PDA sensor was used to detect E2 at a concentration of 15 ng/mL under optimum conditions (pH = 7; incubation time = 10 min; dosage amount = 150 μL). The CDs@MI-PDA showed good reproducibility for determination of E2 with RSDs between 1.8 and 4.8 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical application of the sensor and performance comparison
ce:section.ce:para: The practicality and reliability of the proposed sensor were evaluated by detecting E2 in several types of real samples (e.g., tap water, river water, milk, and chicken). All of the samples were tested before spiking. It was found that E2 was detected in the chicken sample with a value of 0.71 ng/mL. In contrast, the concentrations of E2 in the tap water, river water, and milk samples were all found to be below the detection limit (BDL). Hence, the analysis of all these samples was performed indirectly after spiking with standard solutions of E2 at concentrations of 1, 5, and 10 ng/mL. Satisfactory recoveries for the tap water, river water, and milk samples were obtained with values of 99.9–102.2 % (RSD%: 1.1–1.6), 96.4–99.7 % (RSD%: 1.1–1.3), and 97.0–101.7 % (RSD%: 0.7–1.8), respectively (Table 1 ). These results demonstrate that the proposed sensor could be readily applied for detection of E2 in real samples.

The analytical performance of the proposed CDs@MI-PDA sensor was compared with other reported methods for E2 detection such as electrochemical/fluorescence methods and conventional options like high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Table 2 ). The sensor proposed in this study (CDs@MI-PDA) showed better sensitivity (0.34 ng/mL) than many other reported methods (range: 0.7–28.6 ng/mL). The HPLC determination of E2 in milk after extraction by Fe3O4/MIPs attained a relatively high sensitivity (0.01 ng/mL), although it is based on a complex, time-consuming, multi-step solvent-based treatment procedure and is costly (Gao et al., 2016). The aptamer-based fluorescence resonance energy transfer assay with the aid of thioglycolic acid-capped CdTe quantum dots and cysteamine-stabilized Au nanoparticles also demonstrated an enhanced detection limit of 0.057 ng/mL (Li et al., 2018). However, its response time is moderately longer (18 min) than that of the current sensor (10 min). Moreover, the fluorescence detection of E2 by an aptamer-based assay requires a time-consuming multi-step process. In summary, the CDs@MI-PDA sensor proposed in this study could be employed to achieve highly sensitive detection of E2 with high selectivity and facile operation.

In this research, a new CD-based molecularly-imprinted fluorescent sensor was developed and validated to successfully detect E2 in real samples (e.g., river water, tap water, milk, and chicken samples). The imprinting strategy involves self-polymerization of dopamine around CDs in alkaline solution to effectively simplify the imprinting process. Furthermore, dopamine combined with multifunctional groups, hydrophilicity, and biocompatibility makes it an ideal functional monomer for imprinting. The fluorescent probe showed sensitivity and specificity for E2. Moreover, the current method does not require any complex pre-treatment procedure for E2 detection. In general, the sensor developed in this study is a promising alternative for current methods and is important for E2 detection in environmental monitoring. Moreover, the CDs@MI-PDA-based sensor provides a better sensing platform with enhanced capability of rapid, sensitive, low-cost, green, and selective detection of target analytes. However, some new progress on the polymerization process should be made to help promote the surface imprinting technology. In addition to the common synthesis method of dopamine (e.g., self-polymerization in alkaline condition), its polymerization can also be done by electro-chemical method. The fabrication of PDA film through electropolymerization can be adjusted effectively by the control of current and time. The specific adsorption of PDA layer can be further extended by modification with functionalizing agents. In addition to PDA, some other PDA analogs can also be used for surface imprinting such as poly-norepinephrine, poly-luteolin, and polymer of plant derived phenols. With the new progresses in polymerization techniques, the applicabilities of surface imprinting technology can be expanded to a new horizon.

Shikha Bhogal: Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Irshad Mohiuddin: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Sandeep Kumar: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Ashok Kumar Malik: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Ki-Hyun Kim: Conceptualization, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Kuldeep Kaur: Conceptualization, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors (SB, IM, SK, and AKM) acknowledge the UGC-SAP and Chemistry Department, Punjabi University, Patiala, for providing lab and instrument facilities. AKM thanks the UGC, New Delhi for a Mid-career award grant. KK thanks Mata Gujri College, Fatehgarh Sahib, Punjab for providing lab facilities. KHK acknowledges support made by a grant from the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science and ITC (MSIT) of Korean government (Grant No: 2021R1A3B1068304).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2022.157356.
